item.title: FOCUS I Growth alone won’t keep workers home
item.description: ECONOMIC growth has long been regarded as the ultimate measure of a nation’s success. Rising gross domestic product (GDP), stronger tourism earnings, increased investment and expanding exports have traditionally been viewed as signs of a healthy economy. But according to International Labour Organization (ILO) Head of Education and Training Dr Naren Prasad, Fiji has reached a point where these tra
item.content:encoded:
ECONOMIC growth has long been regarded as the ultimate measure of a nation’s success. Rising gross domestic product (GDP), stronger tourism earnings, increased investment and expanding exports have traditionally been viewed as signs of a healthy economy.

But according to International Labour Organization (ILO) Head of Education and Training Dr Naren Prasad, Fiji has reached a point where these traditional indicators no longer tell the full story.

While delivering his remarks at a public lecture at the University of the South Pacific this week, the Fijian economist argued that the country’s next development challenge is no longer simply growing the economy, but ensuring that growth creates productive jobs, retains skilled workers and improves the lives of ordinary people.

His central message hovered on the central message that Fiji needs to move from a “growth-first” model to a “jobs-first” economy.

Dr Prasad believes Fiji’s labour market has changed more rapidly than its institutions, education system and policy responses.

Thirty years ago, unemployment dominated economic discussions. Today, businesses across multiple sectors report they cannot recruit enough workers despite thousands of young people entering the workforce every year.

Construction firms struggle to find tradespeople. Hospitals cannot retain nurses. Schools are short of teachers. Hotels compete aggressively for staff, while employers increasingly recruit workers from overseas.

At the same time, many young graduates continue to search for meaningful employment that matches their qualifications.

This apparent contradiction is, according to Dr Prasad, evidence that Fiji’s labour market has entered a fundamentally different phase of development rather than facing isolated problems.

One of the strongest parts of Dr Prasad’s lecture was his description of what he called Fiji’s “six labour market paradoxes”.

Although the economy continues to expand, employers increasingly report labour shortages. Economic growth is creating demand for workers faster than the country is producing the skills businesses require.

Fiji has never produced so many school leavers, university graduates and Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) graduates, yet employers continue reporting widespread skills shortages.

The issue, he argues, is no longer educational access but educational relevance.

Employers need digital capability, technical trades, artificial intelligence skills, green technologies, communication skills and problem-solving abilities that are not always aligned with existing training pathways.

The third paradox concerns employment itself.

While unemployment remains a concern for many young people, thousands of vacancies remain unfilled because employers cannot find workers with the required skills.

This highlights the growing mismatch between education outputs and labour market demand.

Perhaps Fiji’s biggest economic challenge today is migration.

Thousands of skilled workers—including nurses, teachers, electricians, engineers and hospitality professionals—continue to leave for Australia, New Zealand and other overseas destinations.

Migration itself is not necessarily negative.

Dr Prasad acknowledged it brings remittances, international experience and global professional networks.

However, the growing outflow of skilled workers has created shortages across critical sectors.

Ironically, while Fiji exports skilled labour, employers are increasingly importing foreign workers to fill vacancies.

Rather than attempting to stop migration, he argues Fiji should focus on becoming a country where talented people want to stay—or eventually return.

Competitive wages, professional development, affordable housing, quality education and stronger public services all influence where skilled people choose to build their futures.

Another major issue highlighted during the lecture was women’s participation in the workforce.

Despite women being more educated than ever before, female labour force participation remains around 36 per cent compared with about 66 per cent for men.

At a time when employers complain about labour shortages, Dr Prasad believes Fiji is overlooking one of its greatest economic resources.

Policies such as affordable childcare, flexible working arrangements, safer transport and equal career opportunities could substantially increase workforce participation while boosting national productivity.

Rather than viewing gender equality solely as a social issue, he argued it should also be regarded as an economic strategy.

item.title: HISTORY I Charlie and the rise of Bau
item.description: DEEP within himself, the Swede Charles Savage —the descendant of the Vikings—felt a quiet satisfaction as he watched the Bau warriors who had been glaring angrily at him gradually avert their eyes. He did not look only around those aboard the sacred canoe. He also cast his gaze towards the occupants of all the other canoes, yet not a single man dared meet his eyes.
item.content:encoded:
DEEP within himself, the Swede Charles Savage —the descendant of the Vikings—felt a quiet satisfaction as he watched the Bau warriors who had been glaring angrily at him gradually avert their eyes.

He did not look only around those aboard the sacred canoe. He also cast his gaze towards the occupants of all the other canoes, yet not a single man dared meet his eyes.

“Let them boast as much as they wish. Let them tell everyone how many men they killed. Had it not been for my help, they would all have died at Nakelo, or at best have fled back to Bau in defeat.”

Once again Savage reflected that, so long as the Vunivalu continued to recognise him and appreciate the value of his firearm, what—or who—did he really have to fear?

While these thoughts occupied his mind and the Bau warriors remained silent, the fleet entered the river mouth before setting course for Bau.

The islands gradually came into view, and with them came the sound of celebration.

The sight of their homeland delighted the warriors, for this was the first occasion on which they could celebrate a victory over a territory belonging to Rewa.

Conversation soon resumed, but it was no longer about boasting as it had been earlier.

Now they spoke only of the festivities that awaited them: the women who would welcome them home, the victory dances that would be performed in honour of the returning warriors, the great feasts that would be prepared, and the days of celebration and rejoicing that would follow.

Stories of individual exploits in battle would be told later, separately, within each clan gathering.

When Savage observed the happiness of the Bau warriors and saw that the expressions of jealousy and suspicion had disappeared from their faces, a feeling of loneliness suddenly overcame him.

He realised that once the celebrations began, he too would once again be forgotten.

When they reached Bau, there would be no one to greet him or recognise him, for he was merely a stranger with neither family nor relatives there.

Then, quite suddenly, his face brightened as he remembered that he did in fact have someone in Bau whom he regarded as family—Lui, the Chinese cook from the ship Elisa.

Savage had almost forgotten Lui amidst the many demands of the previous weeks, after sailing first to Nairai with Daulakeba and then onward to Oneata in search of the new weapon—the European firearm.

Yet ever since the foreign ship had arrived at Nairai, Lui had remained constantly at Savage’s side, looking up to him as his protector and guardian among these formidable people, the Fijians.

During their time in Bau they had become especially close.

As he thought of Lui, Savage said to himself:

“I have been a useless protector, for I have scarcely thought about Lui despite his loyalty to me. When I return to Bau, I shall make every effort to strengthen our friendship, for in this place he truly is my only relative.”

Thus, as they entered Bau amid loud cheers of triumph, the beating of the lali drums and preparations for the victory celebrations, Savage clearly saw how little notice was taken of him.

The people of Bau were greeting only those whom they knew personally, embracing relatives and welcoming one another home.

Savage immediately began searching the crowd for Lui.

He examined every face, looking for the short, dark-complexioned Chinese man.

When they finally disembarked, Savage went straight to the Vunivalu and asked where Lui was.

Ratu Naulivou turned to one of the chiefs who had remained behind to guard the settlement and asked him about the Chinese man.

The chief replied that Lui had been sent, together with several others, to work in the plantations at Tai ko Bau.

A surge of anger came over Savage, and he could no longer restrain himself. He spoke firmly to Ratu Naulivou, insisting that Lui be summoned back immediately.

Because the Vunivalu was in such high spirits after the victory, he did not take offence at Savage’s forceful request. Instead, he immediately gave orders for Savage’s companion, Lui, to be brought back.

The entire island and the settlement buzzed with activity as the Bau warriors dispersed to reunite with their own families.

Money was brought out, ovens were prepared, and everywhere people hurried about making arrangements.

item.title: UNITY I Fiji Muslims, reconciliation and the shared future of Fiji
item.description: THE establishment of Fiji’s Truth and Reconciliation Commission marks an important moment in the country’s democratic journey. It provides an opportunity not only to examine the causes and consequences of political upheaval, but also to strengthen the foundations of a society built on justice, inclusion and mutual respect.
item.content:encoded:
THE establishment of Fiji’s Truth and Reconciliation Commission marks an important moment in the country’s democratic journey.

It provides an opportunity not only to examine the causes and consequences of political upheaval, but also to strengthen the foundations of a society built on justice, inclusion and mutual respect.

Truth and reconciliation cannot succeed by documenting events alone. They require communities to reflect honestly on their experiences, acknowledge the suffering of others and commit themselves to a shared future founded on equal citizenship and human dignity.

The Fiji Muslim community welcomes the opportunity to contribute to this national conversation. For more than one hundred and forty-five years Muslims have regarded Fiji as their permanent home.

Throughout this period, they have lived peacefully alongside indigenous iTaukei, Indo-Fijians and other communities, contributing to the country’s educational, social and economic development while maintaining cordial relations across religious and cultural boundaries.

There is no history of organised communal violence involving Fiji’s Muslims. Instead, the community has consistently sought to build bridges of understanding through education, service and engagement with the wider society.

This submission reflects on the experiences of Muslims during Fiji’s periods of political instability and considers how those experiences can contribute to a broader national dialogue on reconciliation. It also outlines principles that may assist Fiji in building a more cohesive, democratic and inclusive society.

The coups of 1987, the civil unrest surrounding the events of 2000 and the political changes of 2006 affected every sector of Fijian society. Although each event arose under different political circumstances, together they created an enduring sense of uncertainty that altered the social landscape of the nation.

Muslims, like many other communities, experienced these changes not only as political events but as deeply personal disruptions to family life, community institutions and individual aspirations.

Perhaps the most significant consequence was the loss of confidence in Fiji’s long-term stability. Many families who had invested generations in building their lives here concluded that their future was uncertain.

Migration became both a practical response and an emotional decision. Parents sought greater security for their children, professionals accepted opportunities overseas and entire family networks gradually relocated. This movement represented far more than demographic change. It weakened community institutions, disrupted social networks and reduced the pool of experienced leadership available to guide younger generations.

Muslim schools, religious organisations and voluntary associations were particularly affected. Experienced teachers, administrators, community leaders and professionals who had spent decades building these institutions departed for new lives abroad.

Their absence created leadership gaps that required years of rebuilding. Community organisations adapted by developing new leaders and strengthening volunteerism, yet the loss of accumulated experience remained significant.

Economic consequences accompanied these social changes. Farms, businesses and professional practices were abandoned or transferred, while investment decisions increasingly reflected concerns about political uncertainty.

Families that remained in Fiji often found themselves caring for elderly relatives whose children had emigrated. The emotional burden of separation became an often overlooked consequence of migration, affecting family cohesion across generations.

Although the actions of a minority did not reflect the views of the wider population, the events created doubts that persisted well beyond the immediate crisis. For some, those experiences permanently altered their sense of belonging.

Yet time also revealed the resilience of Fiji’s communities. A generation has now grown to adulthood with little direct memory of the events of 1987.

While they recognise the trauma experienced by their parents and grandparents, they are generally more focused on building their own future than reliving the divisions of the past.

The Muslim understanding of reconciliation is grounded in principles that have guided Islamic civilisation since its earliest years. The example most often cited is the establishment of the community in Medina under the leadership of Prophet Muhammad.

Faced with a society divided by tribal conflict and religious diversity, he sought not dominance but coexistence. Different communities retained their own beliefs and traditions while agreeing to principles of justice, mutual responsibility and peaceful coexistence.

This historical experience remains relevant because it demonstrates that lasting peace depends upon institutions that protect diversity while fostering shared citizenship. Social harmony cannot be achieved by erasing differences; it is sustained by recognising diversity as a permanent feature of human society and establishing fair relationships among all communities.

These principles have shaped the experience of Muslims in Fiji. From the earliest settlements, Muslim families established mosques, schools and community organisations that nurtured religious identity while encouraging positive engagement with the wider society.

Fiji was never viewed as a temporary place of residence but as home. Consequently, community development focused not only on preserving faith but also on contributing to the common good of the nation.

Islam teaches that humanity shares a common origin and that diversity among peoples is intended to encourage understanding rather than conflict. Different languages, cultures and traditions are therefore not obstacles to national unity but opportunities for mutual learning. This understanding complements democratic values that recognise the equal dignity of every citizen regardless of ethnicity, religion or cultural background.

Social cohesion is not created through legislation alone. It grows gradually through families, neighbourhoods, schools, places of worship and community organisations. These institutions cultivate the habits of trust, cooperation and shared responsibility that enable diverse societies to flourish.

For Fiji’s Muslims, the family remains the primary institution where these values are learned. Respect for parents, care for elders, responsibility towards children and concern for neighbours form the ethical foundation upon which wider civic responsibility develops. Religious education reinforces these values by emphasising honesty, compassion, justice and service to humanity.

Mosques have traditionally served not only as places of worship but also as centres of education, welfare and community dialogue. Through sermons, classes and public engagement, they have encouraged peaceful coexistence, respect for lawful authority and constructive participation in national life. Religious instruction has consistently emphasised that devotion to God is inseparable from responsibility towards fellow citizens.

Education has played an equally important role in strengthening social cohesion. Muslim schools have operated within Fiji’s national education system while welcoming students from all religious and ethnic backgrounds. Their contribution extends beyond academic achievement. They have provided environments where young people learn to live together peacefully.

Generations of graduates from these schools now serve Fiji as teachers, doctors, engineers, public servants, business leaders, farmers and skilled workers, contributing to every sector of national life.

These investments illustrate an important lesson for reconciliation. Peace is not sustained solely through constitutional arrangements or political settlements. It depends equally upon strong social institutions that cultivate trust, responsibility and shared purpose across generations.

item.title: DISCOVERING FIJI I Guide who led the Viti Levu crossing
item.description: WHEN four European explorers set out to make their first successful crossing of Viti Levu in 1865, history would remember the expedition as the achievement of British Consul Captain Henry M. Jones and his companions. Yet, as Erie Wilson’s account in the Pacific Islands Monthly (October 1967) makes clear, the journey into Fiji’s mountainous interior would have been far more perilous without the kno
item.content:encoded:
WHEN four European explorers set out to make their first successful crossing of Viti Levu in 1865, history would remember the expedition as the achievement of British Consul Captain Henry M. Jones and his companions.

Yet, as Erie Wilson’s account in the Pacific Islands Monthly (October 1967) makes clear, the journey into Fiji’s mountainous interior would have been far more perilous without the knowledge and leadership of John Humphrey Danford – better known as “Harry the Jew.”

Wilson recounts a time when the heart of Viti Levu remained largely unknown to Europeans.

Hostility from the Kaicolo, or mountain people, had frustrated earlier attempts to explore the island, including an expedition sent by Captain Wilkes in 1840.

It would take another 15 years before a successful crossing was achieved.

The expedition was led by Captain Henry M. Jones (V.C)., the British Consul, and included trader F. Hennings, planter J. Boyd and Hamburg naturalist Dr. Graafe.

Also travelling with them was Charlie Lailai, described by Wilson as the Consul’s servant and the son of an early settler, Charlie Pickering.

Their journey began in Levuka aboard the whaleboat of Spanish Frank.

They crossed the reef at Naitasi before travelling up the Rewa river to Nukumoto Island, where they spent their first night at Mr. Storck’s plantation.

The following day brought them to Davis store on the Wainibokasi River, where the expedition gained the man who would become indispensable.

Wilson writes: “John Humphrey Danford (‘Harry the Jew’) joined them as guide and interpreter.”

It was a brief introduction, but one that marked a turning point in the expedition.

After passing through the untouched beauty of Suva Bay, the travellers camped at Eggerton’s cotton plantation on Qara Island before entering the Lobau river.

Their final night before leaving European support was spent among “the blackened ruins of a Namosi village which had been but recently sacked by the Serua people.”

Despite the clear warning of conflict ahead, Captain Jones made a decisive move.

“Captain Jones dismissed ‘Spanish Frank’ and his crew, and, under the guidance of ‘Harry the Jew,’ the five men turned their faces resolutely towards the unknown interior,” Wilson highlights.

From that point, Danford became far more than an interpreter.

He guided the party through country unfamiliar to Europeans and into territories where diplomacy and local knowledge were essential for survival.

The first stages of the overland journey tested every member of the expedition.

Wilson describes them struggling through “a wide swamp at the foot of the ranges,” labouring through mud under intense heat while carrying heavy packs.

Dense jungle soon replaced the swamp, leaving the travellers surrounded by silence, broken only by birds.

“They were without water and to allay thirst were reduced to licking moisture from hanging mosses that covered the boles of the trees,” Wilson writes.

News of their arrival had already spread through what Wilson describes as the bush telegraph.

Runners sent by Ratu Kuruduadua of Namosi met the expedition carrying cooked food, smoked oranges and water.

The following day, exhausted and sore, the Europeans found their burdens eased when their Fijian escort carried their packs through the steep, jungle-covered country.

Each evening more runners arrived with provisions prepared by the Namosi chief.

The group were assisted by the Namosi chief because John Humphrey Danford is Ratu Kuruduadua’s family – being adopted as a brother of the chief.

The expedition eventually descended into the Namosi Valley, where Ratu Kuruduadua welcomed them outside the town’s war fence beneath “an ancient shaddock tree whose branches were festooned with human bones.”

Wilson writes that the chief “received his white visitors ceremoniously, conducted them into his town, and bade his wives attend to the preparation of food with their own hands.”

The explorers remained in Namosi for several days before leaving after hearing reports that two women had been killed by the Laselase people.

item.title: EXPERIENCE I The Garden Island neglect unmasking: Taveuni’s systematics
item.description: TAVEUNI Island, known as the “Garden Island” of Fiji, represents a profound developmental paradox. Spanning 42 kilometres in length and averaging 11 kilometres in width, it is the third-largest island in the archipelago, supporting roughly 17,000 residents. Economically, it is a vital driver of Fiji’s agricultural success, producing over 80 per cent of our export dalo and yaqona, while serving as
item.content:encoded:
TAVEUNI Island, known as the “Garden Island” of Fiji, represents a profound developmental paradox. Spanning 42 kilometres in length and averaging 11 kilometres in width, it is the third-largest island in the archipelago, supporting roughly 17,000 residents.

Economically, it is a vital driver of Fiji’s agricultural success, producing over 80 per cent of our export dalo and yaqona, while serving as a key engine for tourism revenue.

Yet, for those who call this island home, the “Garden Island” is a landscape of profound institutional neglect.

While Suva’s corridors of power prioritise the mainland, Taveuni’s southern districts—Vuna, Navakawau, Ura, Waimaqera, and Salialevu—languish in systemic deprivation.

This is not a series of unfortunate accidents; it is the predictable outcome of decades of governance viewing Taveuni as a resource to be extracted rather than a community to be nurtured.

The crisis facing Taveuni is a matter of political will. Across successive administrations—from the FijiFirst government to the current Coalition—the pattern of neglect remains constant.

During the Bainimarama era, residents endured a decade of transport crises, including chronic bus service suspensions from 2016–2018 caused by failing infrastructure and regulatory indifference.

Today, the script has merely changed titles. We have moved from bus suspensions to total supply chain collapse.

The July 2026 shipping crisis was the inevitable consequence of a government that failed to proactively manage the maritime regulatory environment.

Rather than protecting the status quo by resolving licensing and crewing disputes in a timely manner, the state allowed these issues to escalate, causing a preventable collapse in the delivery of essential fuel and food.

The supply chain collapse of July 2026 serves as a definitive case study in governance failure.

When shipping lines Goundar Shipping and Interlink halted voyages over regulatory disputes with the Maritime Safety Authority of Fiji (MSAF)—specifically regarding the failure to approve experienced international crew members to address the domestic deficit of Class 1 and 2 Boat Masters—Taveuni was effectively placed under siege.

This was not a natural disaster; it was a regulatory one. MSAF’s failure to resolve seafarer licensing issues, coupled with the government’s lack of contingency plans, brought the island to its knees.

That an island generating the bulk of our export revenue can be cut off for weeks demonstrates a complete absence of institutionalized emergency reserves.

The Chaudhari intervention: A damning indictment

When fuel pumps ran dry and the government remained silent, private citizen Rohil Chaudhari, owner of Suncity Supermarket, was forced to act.

As the island faced total paralysis, Chaudhari personally secured 14,000 litres of mixed fuel via a private barge that arrived on July 7, 2026. Because a fire had destroyed his station’s automated pumps, his staff manually distributed the fuel using measured containers—a gruelling, archaic process.

Customers travelled over 20 kilometres, some from as far as Lavena Village, to secure a 20-litre ration to keep livelihoods alive.

That a private merchant had to step into the role of the state is a searing critique of our national leadership. It highlights a dangerous precedent: when the state abdicates its primary duty, citizens are left to fight for survival alone.

The shipping freeze did more than empty supermarket shelves; it crippled Taveuni’s export economy. As vessels remained docked in Suva, hundreds of tonnes of dalo and kava destined for overseas markets began to spoil.

Farmers in Vuna, who rely on these exports, were left with rotting crops and zero income. Simultaneously, resorts at peak occupancy faced the harrowing prospect of guest evacuations as generator fuel evaporated.

That our national export revenue is being sacrificed because of a bureaucratic spat between MSAF and private operators is not just incompetence; it is economic sabotage. The systemic failure to manage maritime logistics directly undermines our national post-pandemic recovery.

The water paradox: A monument to incompetence

Perhaps the most egregious symbol of this neglect is the Navaca Desalination Plant. Built in 2015 at a cost exceeding $1 million, it failed within months due to operational ineptitude.

Today, it remains a rusted tomb for taxpayer funds while 4,200 residents in the south survive on a “fractured” water-carting regime. This is a government that can find millions for vanity projects but cannot provide a consistent, gravity-fed water supply to the very farmers who prop up our national economy.

Residents scavenge from contaminated sources—a reality that would be deemed a national scandal if it occurred in the Suva-Nausori corridor.

The failure to address this, despite the 2017 Israel-aided hydrological survey confirming the viability of a gravity-fed project at Salialevu, is an insult to every citizen in the south.

The state’s failure to provide a reticulated water system creates a compounding cycle of destruction.

Because the government refuses to build pipelines, it relies on heavy, multi-ton water-carting trucks to service the southern districts.

These trucks, operating daily, pulverise the unsealed South Coast Road, rendering it a gauntlet of potholes.

item.title: COMMUNITY OUTREACH I Discovering the heart of Fiji
item.description: THE laughter of children echoed through village schools as a group of teenagers from Christchurch, New Zealand traded familiar classrooms for a very different lesson in Fiji. For 10 days, 15 students and teachers from Aidanfield Christian School travelled beyond the country’s well-known tourist destinations, visiting schools and villages, sharing gifts, performing together and building friendships
item.content:encoded:
THE laughter of children echoed through village schools as a group of teenagers from Christchurch, New Zealand traded familiar classrooms for a very different lesson in Fiji.

For 10 days, 15 students and teachers from Aidanfield Christian School travelled beyond the country’s well-known tourist destinations, visiting schools and villages, sharing gifts, performing together and building friendships that crossed cultures.

What began as a service trip quickly became a journey of learning and connection, leaving both visitors and hosts with memories they hope will last long after the trip ended.

Head of Middle School Teresa Thomson said the visit marked an important milestone for the school.

“It’s our school’s first time working with Graeme and Jane Mitchell to come with them to Fiji, but we are hoping that it will become an ongoing thing where we can build a relationship that the school has developed through the Fiji Missions with the villages and the schools that we have visited,” she said.

The group of 15 students was selected a year before departure, giving them plenty of time to prepare.

“There has been a lot of time fundraising, working really hard to try offset the cost to come,” Ms Thomson said.

“We have worked as a team to develop our gifts and our talents so that we have something to perform and present to those that we visit.”

The preparations also included collecting donations after being advised that schools would appreciate picture books, sports equipment and stationeries.

“We brought those things with us. Some of the students purchased things to bring, others gathered donated goods from their network community and supporters, and Graeme and Jane had some.”

While the donations were welcomed, Ms Thomson said the greatest value of the trip could not be packed into a suitcase.

“We have enjoyed the warmth of the people, and our students have made connection with students here,” she said.

“We call it a service trip – it’s just been a wonderful networking time together.”

For Ms Thomson, the experience encouraged students to reflect on their own lives and faith.

“Our message and something that the students will have to think about is what has given value to them, how have they grown, how has this helped their faith to grow, their understanding of the world, for them to appreciate their life at home, what they can give.”

The team was guided by the Bible verse 1 Timothy 4:12: “Don’t let anyone look down on you because you are young, but set an example for the believers in speech, in conduct, in love, in faith and in purity.”

For Amara Hoffman, a Year 10 student at Aidanfield Christian School, Fiji had always been a holiday destination of sandy beaches and island resorts but stepping into the heart of a Fijian village opened her eyes to a side of the country she had never experienced before.

Joining a community outreach trip for the first time, Amara said the experience had been both meaningful and unforgettable.

“It’s been really great to socialise with the students and villagers,” she said.

“It’s definitely a new experience from what we’re used to at home, but it’s been great going out and seeing the smiles on the children’s faces and just connecting with them in ways that we wouldn’t usually at home.”

Although she had visited Fiji before, Amara said this trip was unlike any other.

“It’s my first time being a part of something like this. I’ve been to Fiji before, but not in the heartland. I’ve been to resorts, but I like this experience better,” she said.

She said what stood out most was the warmth and generosity of the Fijian people.

“I found that the Fijian people are very welcoming and their hospitality is really good. They gave us lots even when they didn’t have much themselves, which I was thankful for.”

As the group visited villages, Amara was fascinated by the close-knit way communities lived together.

She said village life was something rarely seen in New Zealand, making the experience even more special.

“It was cool seeing how Fijians live in their communities. Villages are not much of a thing in New Zealand and being able to experience that was really cool because it showed how families are closely knitted together.”

For Amara, the trip became more than just a visit to Fiji. It was an opportunity to build genuine connections, learn from another culture, and discover that some of the country’s greatest treasures are found not in its resorts, but in the kindness of its people and the strength of its communities.

For Lennox Spain, the past 10 days in Fiji have been unlike anything he had experienced before.

The trip gave him an opportunity to grow in his faith while discovering what he described as the “real Fiji” beyond the places most visitors see.

item.title: HEALTH I Health juice with a spiritual purpose
item.description: The phenomenon and the surge in the number of wellness initiatives centered on blended health juices and detox drinks reflects a broader shift toward preventive health and lifestyle-focused nutrition. As people become more conscious of diet-related illnesses, busy schedules, and the desire to feel healthier, juices made from fruits, vegetables, herbs, and functional ingredients have gained widespr
item.content:encoded:
The phenomenon and the surge in the number of wellness initiatives centered on blended health juices and detox drinks reflects a broader shift toward preventive health and lifestyle-focused nutrition.

As people become more conscious of diet-related illnesses, busy schedules, and the desire to feel healthier, juices made from fruits, vegetables, herbs, and functional ingredients have gained widespread popularity.

Social media influencers, wellness brands, and celebrity endorsements have further accelerated this trend by promoting juice cleanses and detox programs as simple, accessible ways to improve energy, digestion, and overall well-being.

For many Fijian consumers, these products symbolise a commitment to healthier living and provide a convenient way to increase fruit and vegetable intake.

Aside from the fight against NCDs, several factors have drawn people to the health juice movement. Modern lifestyles often involve highly processed foods, sedentary work, and stress, leading many to seek quick and tangible solutions for improving their health.

Marketing and social media frequently emphasises “cleansing” the body of toxins, boosting immunity, aiding weight loss, and restoring balance.

Brightly colored juices, natural ingredients, and attractive branding reinforce perceptions of purity and vitality.

Psychological factors also play a role: participating in a detox program can create a sense of control, motivation, and a fresh start, especially after periods of unhealthy eating or during seasonal health campaigns.

Despite their popularity, many claims associated with health juices are not strongly supported by scientific evidence.

In healthy individuals, the body’s liver, kidneys, lungs, and digestive system already perform the functions of removing waste products and metabolizing potentially harmful substances.

While juices can provide valuable vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants, there is little evidence that juice cleanses remove toxins more effectively than the body’s natural processes.

Many commercial juices are also high in natural sugars and low in dietary fiber because the juicing process removes much of the fruit and vegetable pulp.

Extended juice-only cleanses may result in inadequate protein, healthy fats, and other essential nutrients, pot`entially leading to fatigue, muscle loss, or unstable blood sugar levels in some individuals.

A balanced perspective recognizes that freshly prepared juices can be a useful complement to a healthy diet when consumed in moderation, particularly as a convenient way to increase vegetable and fruit intake.

However, many feel they should not be viewed as a substitute for balanced meals or as a scientifically proven method of detoxification.

Sustainable health outcomes are more consistently associated with regular physical activity, adequate sleep, a varied diet rich in whole foods, proper hydration, and evidence-based medical guidance rather than short-term detox regimens.

Wellness initiatives that encourage these broader lifestyle habits are likely to provide more lasting benefits than those focused solely on juice cleanses.

The Seventh-day Adventist Church has been a major force and strong advocate for healthy living.

According to the church, Adventists’ belief in heath is guided by four foundational principles which are;

1. Inspiration: The belief that the Word of God is the very best guide to making wise choices in every area of life and health. Scripture speaks eloquently on a wide variety of health topics and reveals a loving God who wants to give His children life “more abundantly)” (John 10:10);

2. Evidence: Advenstists operate under the conviction that God is the author of all true science. Therefore, followers respect evidence-based health science which stands up to the rigors of careful examination and analysis according to accepted scientific principles;

3. Balance: Because humans are wholistic beings, a balanced approach in life is essential to health. This involves avoiding harmful practices and using wisely and carefully even the best things in life. Even good things can be taken to excess. Therefore, adventists are deeply committed to physical, mental, social, and spiritual balance to support health, happiness, and healing; and

4. Relationships: Social and spiritual support are essential to health and human flourishing. Cultivating positive relationships with God, family members, friends, and community members will play a significant role in our personal health, productivity, and ability to help others.

Some time back, a few families who travel from afar to attend church on Saturday planned to provide breakfast not only for themselves but for anyone who arrives at church between 7:00–8:45 a.m., just in time for the first segment of worship, Sabbath School, begins at 9:00 a.m.

This was also done to encourage members to arrive on time.

“On that same Sabbath, the local church health department had independently planned to launch a weekly health juice initiativ,” said Vugalei Adventist Church deputy youth leader Sakeasi Tuni.

“This was in response to the increasing cases of flu and other illnesses affecting both children and adults, as well as the rising prevalence of diabetes.”

So for the first six Sabbaths (Saturdays), these initiatives were carried out in front of the church near the Vugalei roundabout, primarily serving local residents and drivers dropping off members.

“It was not until the annual Global Youth Day of the SDA Church on March 21 this year that the youth department decided to combine both initiatives, take full responsibility for them, and move the outreach to the roadside where members continue to serve every Saturday morning,” Tuni said.

The gospel is shared organically through intentional hospitality and relationship building rather than cold-calling. By first meeting a tangible human need, youths create a judgment-free space to build trust before diving into more complex spiritual narratives.

item.title: REFLECTION I The eternal equation that has not been permitted to receive rest through time
item.description: Is universal creation based upon mathematical and scientific principles and is there evidence in existence of this probability? This poem attempts to provide of the answer. It was inspired by the observations of nature – that her serene silence – resonates with the subtle and sublime geometric signs and symbols of science in creation.
item.content:encoded:
Is universal creation based upon mathematical and scientific principles and is there evidence in existence of this probability?

This poem attempts to provide of the answer. It was inspired by the observations of nature – that her serene silence – resonates with the subtle and sublime geometric signs and symbols of science in creation.

Research world-wide has proven that the roots of science are factually found in nature. The universe is vast and varied and within this vastness, there is depth and divinity in all creations.

For those who choose to look and listen, the eternal equation can be well resolved by the existence and evidence of nature.

Consider the silky spider web spun in symmetrical strength and structure. The buds that bloom on time per season. The logarithmic spiral design of a snail shell. The calculus curves of a clover leaf in a mirror reflection matched to pure perfection. The hexagon honeycomb home of bees.

Also take into account the sunflower that sets her gaze in loyalty upon the light. Her petals in radiant harmony with the solar energy of the sun. The farm – macy of herbs in the flower fields that have healed beings through time. The rooster that beckons all from sweet slumber at the first sight of sunlight. The birds that sing as the sky is adorned with the soft shades of dawn.

The essence of Sacred Geometry of course, is that all of creation under the tender care of nature is sacred. If the Third Eye (chakra) observes life on earth, it well reveals the definite, infinite and divine presence of science in creation.

The Golden Ratio – a mathematical constant that exists in nature, in the human body, and even in artwork. It is a foundation for the principle of sacred geometry and the concealed order of the universe.

The Fibonacci Sequence – embodies the Golden Ratio and is a reflection of universal order and the harmonious beauty and balance of life upon earth.

The eternal question is addressed by mother nature. To bear witness to the presence and principle of sacred science, look no further than the flower fields; the meadows in bloom; the forest of trees; the garden beaming with brilliant blooms and alive with life; the hills of green; the sand of a golden hue and the waters that we ford – rivers, streams and lakes that merge to meet the ocean blue.

All creatures and creations in the care of nature resonate with energy and vibrate at a divine frequency, that heals and harmonises life upon earth. It is a matter of sheer shame and deep distress when people are not aware; not vigilant; not caring, cautious and concerned of their natural environment in their rush and race for material acquisitions and accompanying accolades; that they tragically diminish and destroy their own energy field; their own balance with existence when they curse and condemn elements of nature as a hindrance in their life journey.

Actions against nature in this age of “modernity” are applauded as development when in reality, many people are engaged in destruction.

I recently witnessed a house under renovation and noticed in sadness a fallen tree as old as time beside the young house owner and his property developer. They stood in deep discussion beside the fallen tree, with pride and plans to construct and complete a compound fence project. It was a devastating sight to see – not just the sight of a tree of life fallen but the absence of any care or concern by the people present. Their callous action for material development, which will not sustain life for eternity by the very nature of being a material creation – desecrated the divinity of nature itself.

The tree was obviously in existence before the young home owner was present on earth and alas, the tree was later discarded rather than re-planted or preserved in her original location; such was the hindrance that it was viewed as, rather than the harmonious balance the presence of the tree actually brought to the property.

In contrast, a homeowner was not in a position to replace an aged roof for almost a decade. Within that period, it transformed into a home for birds seeking sanctuary from the winds and rain or to build their nests and bring forth new life upon earth. The roof was mended in due time but the trees planted in the vicinity served a purpose parallel to the aged roof.

Beware the deeds one engages in for material gain – do you engage in the development or destruction of the sacred and sustainable gifts of nature that strive towards the continuity of life on earth.

Deliberate destruction lowers the energy, vibration, frequency and aura of human existence on earth.

The blessed presence and purpose of sacred geometry has been redefined, redesigned, realigned and reversed rather than yield the results a person aspires for.

Hence, nature is at liberty to restore, retain, retaliate, preserve and protect her kingdom.

The plants you attend to will bloom with beauty. Likewise, the plants you neglect will wither and wilt before your negligent gaze. The trees that offered shade and shelter will warn other trees before it is butchered with brute force in the disguise of development.

Seek the blessings of water that falls upon earth to sustain and maintain life. The water you consume also absorbs energy around you. Your words in the presence of water is the energy you inhale.

The creatures small and sacred that people may consider a nuisance and are displaced were the true homeowners of the locality. It was their habitat that was destroyed for development. Do not equate their silence and/or disappearance as approval for one’s deliberately destructive actions.

The earth will match the energy of destruction with her energy of rejuvenation and reconstruction without fear or favour.

There is no fine line between development and destruction. It is a defined and definite line that people choose to cross over. If the retaliation by Nature is as blatant and brutal, then the Wheel of Dharma – the Wheel of Life – has completed the duty it is committed to.

People aspire for the sanctuary of their eternal home – the heavens – yet their trial and test is upon earth – a miniature replica of the eternal Home.

Sacred geometry for the eye that sees from the birds and bees to the streams and hills of lush trees is the key to a harmonious existence on earth.

People tamper with the balance of life by their own disharmonious decisions and deeds.

Time is an essence. Beware her presence. Learn the lessons and blessed be your legacy to life upon earth.

Indeed, an attitude of gratitude to nature and her sacred geometry is the essence of life for the works and wonders of nature are an equation akin to the divine frequency; to the universal vibration of sacred geometry. A testament to the truth of life – The nature of nature is collaborative and not competitive and her existence is evidence of the miraculous continuity of life.

Picture: WWW.PEXELS.COM/Nguyen Ngoc Tien
